skill.name: azure-storage-queue-py
skill.description: Azure Queue Storage SDK guidance for Python applications that use Azure queues for asynchronous messaging, task distribution, and worker processing.
skill.summary: Use Azure Queue Storage from Python with authenticated clients, queue operations, message handling, async clients, and encoding patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell execution patterns, hardcoded URLs, and one weak cryptography rule. Human review found documentation examples only: Markdown code fences, placeholder Azure account URLs, and front matter text, with no executable malware or prompt injection evidence.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer treated Markdown backtick fences and example blocks as Ruby or shell execution. The lines contain installation and Python SDK documentation examples, not executable skill logic, so this is a false positive with low residual risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every reported external command location maps to a Markdown code fence or fenced example in SKILL.md. No evidence found that the skill executes these commands automatically.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Azure URLs in Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are placeholder Azure Queue Storage endpoints used to show account URL formatting. They do not target an unknown third-party service or exfiltrate local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs use the standard Azure Queue Storage domain with a placeholder account name. The context is SDK client setup, not hidden network behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Rule Matched Front Matter
low_findings.description: The high static finding at line 3 points to the YAML description block marker. No weak cryptographic algorithm or cryptographic operation appears at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is front matter for the skill description and contains no crypto API, hash algorithm, cipher, or key handling. This directly contradicts the static rule label.

content.user_title: Build Azure Queue workflows in Python
content.value_statement: Queue-based Python apps need reliable setup patterns. This skill gives Azure Queue Storage examples for clients, messages, async use, and operational practices.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install azure-storage-queue and azure-identity for Python projects.
Demonstrates DefaultAzureCredential authentication with QueueServiceClient and QueueClient.
Covers queue creation, deletion, listing, metadata, and property inspection.
Explains sending, receiving, peeking, updating, deleting, and clearing messages.
Includes async client usage for higher throughput worker patterns.
Shows binary message handling with Azure Queue base64 policies.
limitations: It provides examples and guidance, not a complete production queue service.
It requires a valid Azure Storage account and configured Azure credentials.
It does not implement poison queue handling beyond dequeue count guidance.
It does not cover every Azure authentication mode or advanced Service Bus features.

use_cases.title: Add background task queues
use_cases.description: Create Python producers and workers that send, receive, and delete Azure Queue Storage messages safely.

use_cases.title: Review queue operations
use_cases.description: Check common SDK calls for queue lifecycle management, visibility timeouts, metadata, and monitoring reads.

use_cases.title: Prototype async processing
use_cases.description: Use async Azure Queue clients to test high-throughput message processing patterns before production design.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic queue client
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new Python project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to show the basic Python setup for Azure Queue Storage with DefaultAzureCredential and a queue client.

prompt_templates.title: Send and process messages
prompt_templates.scenario: Build a producer and worker flow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline how a Python producer sends queue messages and a worker receives, processes, and deletes them.

prompt_templates.title: Tune visibility and retries
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to explain how visibility timeout, time to live, and dequeue count should be handled in a Python worker.

prompt_templates.title: Design an async queue worker
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design an async Azure Queue Storage worker pattern for Python, including message cleanup and operational checks.

output_examples.input: I need to process uploaded files in the background with Python.
output_examples.output: Use a queue message for each upload, receive messages in a worker, delete each message after successful processing, and track repeated failures with dequeue count.

output_examples.input: How should I inspect a queue without changing worker behavior?
output_examples.output: Use message peeking for monitoring because it reads message content without changing visibility or reserving messages for processing.

output_examples.input: When should I use the async client?
output_examples.output: Use the async client when a worker needs concurrent I/O and higher throughput, especially when message processing waits on network services.

best_practices: Delete messages only after successful processing to avoid silent data loss.
Set visibility timeout to match expected work duration and retry behavior.
Monitor dequeue count so repeated failures can be routed or investigated.
anti_patterns: Do not leave processed messages in the queue after successful work.
Do not use a very short visibility timeout for long-running jobs.
Do not store sensitive payloads in queue messages without an approved protection plan.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you use Azure Queue Storage from Python for message queues, workers, and asynchronous processing.

faq.question: Does it require Azure credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples use DefaultAzureCredential, so your environment must provide valid Azure identity configuration.

faq.question: Can it create and delete queues?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance includes queue creation, deletion, listing, metadata, and property operations.

faq.question: Does it cover asynchronous clients?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes async QueueClient usage for sending, receiving, and deleting messages.

faq.question: Does it replace Azure Service Bus?
faq.answer: No. It notes that Service Bus is better for advanced features such as sessions and topics.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: The audit found documentation examples only and no malicious behavior. It is low risk because it references networked Azure services.
